name: unbounded-growth-has-two-independent-dimensions
tags: checkpoint, unbounded growth, cross-thread eviction, intra-thread versions, session cap, row cap, langgraph, sqlite bloat
body:
A retention/eviction policy that caps "how many concurrent things exist" (e.g. active sessions/threads) does not automatically cap "how much history accumulates within one still-active thing" — these are independent growth dimensions, and a fix for one silently leaves the other unbounded.

**Why:** `_trim_checkpoints()` correctly capped concurrent Claude Code sessions (evicts whole inactive thread_ids) from day one, but nothing bounded how many checkpoint VERSIONS a single long-running thread accumulates — LangGraph's SqliteSaver writes one full-snapshot row per graph step and keeps every version by design (time-travel/resumability). One unusually long conversation (this session, 2026-07-05) reached 5976 checkpoint rows / 447k writes rows / 1.68GB before `get_current_session()`'s `checkpointer.list()` started hanging 10s+. The bug was invisible for a long time because it only bites the rare long-running-session case, not the common one the original cap was designed around.

**How to apply:** When designing any cap/eviction policy, explicitly ask both "how many concurrent instances am I bounding?" and "how much history within one instance am I bounding?" — don't assume capping one automatically bounds the other. Also: when adding a NOT-IN-based cleanup query (e.g. orphaned `writes` rows), prefer "delete anything not currently in the retained set" over "delete only rows tied to what I'm evicting right now" — the latter misses pre-existing orphans that predate the fix (47,652 distinct checkpoint_ids in `writes` vs only 30 in `checkpoints`, most already orphaned before this session's fix ran).
